speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Clarice Moody Sampson took place at her home in law school on November 16, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: He started buying one sack of flour. He'd buy a barrel for a sack. So he had a great big container made that was in the butt tree. Great big square box and the lead just down in. You could already get it out. That was filled up with flour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before the new flour came in, I think that, or did he want the new flour in the fall?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I can't remember what the rule was. And sugar by the hundred pounds. So I think that was just that instinct system.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he was that way because he had been close to not having enough time to lose the hour?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think probably. He ran away from home when he was 13 and went on his own. He went to the family living near St. John, New Brunswick. He went down to Boston and that kid was on his own. So I think, I don't know how he fared, he never would talk about it to us. So maybe there were pretty difficult times. Also, I know he'd never let us go barefoot, even in the summer on the grass, because we'd stub our toes. So evidently he went without shoes at times. But mama let us take off our shoes sometimes in the afternoon, but we sure had to get our feet washed and our shoes on before our dad came home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he didn't even know about it then?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, evidently not.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: So I'll bet you really wanted to go without shoes. Oh yes, the hot days, you know. But we had more in sidewalks and you could stub your toes so easily, you know. You know, you said to Laura that sometimes in your family you said that the boys were closer to mama and the girls were closer to mom. It seemed that way to the girls in the family and maybe, I don't know whether she actually, but it just seemed to me that mama always favored the boys a little. Of course, they were all older than I and really not the one to judge. Well, it wouldn't be unusual, I don't think, if it was true, because it seems to me that the girls often did seem closer to their father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel close to him?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Very.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think I was quite the apple of his eye when I was a little girl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I know my mother used to tell—Am I on?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yeah, I'd tell you. My mother used to tell about when my oldest sister was born. They lived back in Missouri. But Sunday she'd dress Mabel all up and dad would take her down when he went down to get his mail, you know, at the post office. And he was quite proud to take away his little daughter down. But I know after my brother older than I, who was five years older than I, died, I think my mother's health was never very good again. I think it just crushed her. He died in the Philippines. But then, you know, I think dad, my oldest brother was over there working for the government. And Morris was like, a lot of kids aren't out. He didn't want to get out. He didn't want to be tied down to school. He was only in prep school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When he left?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Mm -hmm. But my dad let him go because Albert said he could come over to the Philippines and he'd see that he was okay to Manoa. You told Laura how he died. It sounds like it was that climate and it might have been really hard on Americans. Well it was because my oldest brother came home. Almost broken health. Well did your mother, you mentioned to Laura the operation that she had which was pretty serious on her colon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After that, did she still go out visiting?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh yes. Oh yes. She wasn't in the living room. No, no, she was active in church. But she just couldn't do all the things that a lot of women and mothers did. She just took her strength and warned that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the kinds of things are like house cleaners?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yeah, that sort of thing. That's just what I mean, physical work. She couldn't do that.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But her church work and so on, she could keep on with that. Although she didn't go as much, I don't believe.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But it never seemed to affect her. She loved company and I know our minister's little daughters used to come out and she'd sit and play games with them. That was when I was too grown up to do that. She did so much of that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was cleanliness really important in your family?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes. My father was very fastidious.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Before bathtub days, the bath was taken, sponge bath. We had a big bath pan that we used.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Sunday morning, dad used that bath pan in the kitchen after everybody had gone to Sunday school and so on. But he'd go to church, you see. He'd get his toilet over. I can remember so well when he used the shave. He'd add it to his towel and he'd spread on the dresser and put all of his paraphernalia out. Then nothing was ever left out. He always cleaned up and put everything away. He was very fastidious. Of course, in those days, men wore long underwear and so on. It was such a trial to get things dry.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: We had clothes racks and things and the clothes were hung on the porch and so on. It finally got dry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the girls do that after your mother was sick?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I tell you, there's a period in there I just don't quite realize how we got along. I know at one time, my sister, older than I, the second one older than I, was teaching here in the Moscow schools and I was in high school, I presume.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes. Our clothes were sent out, the laundry. Where they were sent, the husband must have spoke the pipe because they'd come home just smelling so of tobacco. Then of course, after that, we started sending all the flatwork, the sheets, the towels, everything like that to the laundry. Mr. Green, by then, his laundry was—of course, before that, he'd— be devouring, but I don't think our washing ever went down there when it was done and washed up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your father concerned, say, about the furniture not having any dust on it?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes. Clean. In his marble works, in the showroom, especially if he was showing a monument or showing monuments to customers, out'd come his handkerchief and all the time he was showing them, he'd be using his handkerchief to wipe them off. But his method of dusting was a feather duster. Of course, the dust settled and there was a lot of marble dust in the shop from Macarvey. But oh, he was very—no dust, no anything around. In fact, in our house, every morning, even the cleaning days, where there was a cleaning day at once a week, then everything was—oh boy, and they used to sweep and the duster drove out. We always hoped for a wind from the east on sweeping days so it would all go out the front door again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did everybody take part in that?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no, no. I don't remember my mother ever sweeping, but my older sisters. And then finally, after they were away from home, if we didn't have help, I'd do it. And I remember my mother thought it was pretty heavy work for me and she would do so many little things for my benefit because I had done all that hard work. And I could remember one of our bedrooms upstairs before we had the bathroom had stoves in it. And that's where the girls always took their baths. And I could remember on Saturday when I was growing up, after the girls were away from home teaching my sisters, after I'd cleaned the house, I'd have my bath and go to the someone's house or go do something, go to town or do something. And my mother would empty my bath water and do all that because I'd worked hard in the morning.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, the work that you had to do was in the sweeping, you had to lift everything up and make sure everything was clean under your pensioners and all that. Oh yes, the way our house was. The master bedroom was off of the living room and the chairs were all carved in there while the dining room and the living room were swept because the dust would just envelop you. But every morning the thing was around the heater in the living room, you took a little whisk room in the dust pan and cleaned all that up so that that general cleaning didn't go on only once a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have rugs on the floor or something?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh yes, carpets, all the wall carpets we had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh you did?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes. So they'd have to be swept. Mm -hmm. Swept too. But I don't think probably they got as dirty as lots of people's carpets did because we all came in the kitchen door unless our rubbers or boots or so on. And my father was a kind. He never went outside without wiping his feet on the rug at the back door or anything. So I don't think the cleaning was as hard in our home maybe as it was in some other homes that had to clean in that way. Well you know you mentioned eating in the dining room and not in the kitchen. Treating us a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that's what most Moscow families did at that time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Because I know in the country they always ate in the kitchen it seems. Well I really can't say. Some of them I am. Well no I didn't think of two friends. One friend or father was a doctor. They always ate in the dining room. And they usually had a hired girl. Another friend, her father was a blacksmith. And they always ate in the kitchen because he'd come from the shop and he'd wash up and they'd eat in the kitchen. I don't remember of ever eating in the dining room at their house ever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made it nicer for you to eat in the kitchen?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Because you said you really liked it in the mariches. Oh I don't know I just saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just cozy or because it was smaller and closer?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well our kitchen was big but it just seemed to me, well maybe just because it was different. But I just thought that was, and it wasn't breakfast it would be Sunday evening supper. I never remember of, and even when, after I was married and my sister was keeping house for my father, they never ate out on a table in the kitchen. There was a table there that they could have eaten on. There was this work table with the bins bought and so on. And there was another table that sat over by the other kitchen door where the groceries were put when they were delivered and so on. But that was kept clear. They could have eaten there. But they just never did it. Meals were always served in the dining room. So really the only use of the kitchen was pretty much to prepare meals. Yes and the laundry. And laundry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you visit at all in the kitchen?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Only if for some reason we ate out there Sunday evening and sat around. They opened the oven door and sat around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So usually you would visit in the living room or the dining room?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Either one. Always. But not so much around the table maybe as we did in the living room. We just went to the living room.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And it wasn't a parlor, it was a living room. Front room we called it. But it wasn't in any sense a parlor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the difference be between your living room and a parlor?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, now some friends of mine, the binds were kept pulled and there was no heat in there only from the occasion they knew they were going to use the parlor. And it was just a dressed up room. And they did have nice things in there. But I know when I used to visit that friend I stayed all night there quite a bit when I was a kid. We'd go in there once in a while and just kind of look around with a little awe maybe. And sell them used. And sell them used.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But in your living room, in your family, what did you have? Was it mostly chairs? Was there a sofa?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh no. The library table that Harry has there and there. And rockers. We had rockers and the piano and a couch. We called it a lounge, kind of like that painting thing at the museum.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But our furniture wasn't grand but it was very comfortable. And never chosen as a set or anything. Every piece had just been bought as my father could afford to buy it. And then sometimes we got new chair and the old rocking chair was put upstairs in somebody's room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In your family, did your father show affection to the kids much or was that considered?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No. I don't think there was ever a night when I was a kid or my father was too tired that I didn't climb in his lap while he read his paper as tired as he was. And his favorite word for calling us was darling. That's really nice. Because I have the idea that some families, parents didn't feel they ought to show affection to the kids. Well, it wasn't so in our family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do much to please your father? When you were a kid, did you feel motivated by that?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I think I probably told that after my brother Morris was away from home and he did the sprinkling and mowing the lawn and so on. I fell out of that job because that's why I never learned to cook because I did those things. But I know I used to be in a hurry to get off someplace or something and I didn't do very good sprinkling. But I made it look awful wet on the surface so dad would think I'd done a good job of sprinkling. But I don't think I ever thought much about whether I was pleasing my father only in little things like that. But I'm sure I didn't try to displease him at all. And from the time I was, well, when my sisters were away from home teaching and my mother couldn't go downtown with me to shop and so on, I was just put on my own to buy my own clothes. And I was never told you can't get this or you can't get that. I knew about what I was expected to spend and I stayed with Inbound when I was a teenager with my clothes. I remember one time though I got a coat and boy, I got sick of it because we usually wore our coats two years, our winter coats. But I'd chosen it. It was what I wanted. And nothing was said at the time that maybe I'd get tired of it, but I surely did. I remember so well when I was a little girl, real little. And the family pocketbook wasn't so big. My mother took me down to Creighton store and I was going to have a new winter coat. And Mr. Creighton, I think he was very fond of my mother and he came, I think, while a sale was being there showing us and me. And this little red coat, I can just still see it, pretty red coat and it had a velvet collar. Oh, I thought it was so beautiful, but Mama thought it was really more. And she could pay and as I remember it just about picked me then there wasn't any room for growth for the next year, you know. And Mr. Creighton came along and said, well, she looks pretty nice in that. I think she should have it. I and her mama decided to buy the red coat and I don't remember how long I wore it or anything, but I can just still see that red coat with the red velvet collar. That's nice. He was pretty kind to Creighton. Oh, yes. We always thought, of course, eating in the dining room on white tablecloths, they wore out, you know, and every year that the red tag sale, they had it in those days, too. Mama would go in and buy a certain number of sheets and tablecloths, you know, for the year. And she and Mr. Creighton went to Ireland every year and bought linen. That's a lot of my linen. It was all from a Creighton store that was in my cedar chest.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And he just delighted, so in showing Mama the linen he got in Ireland, you know. And then every Christmas he always sent her a great big box of candy, and we always thought that was nice, but I was good. You know, I wanted to ask you some about the way that more prominent families were in Moscow. The Creighton's are probably won't be considered one of the... Well, yes, in a way, but she never mixed Mrs. Creighton. And some of her friends that she'd go to see were some of the most humble people in Moscow. The shoemakers' wife and the neighbors beyond us, we kind of looked at them as poor white trash, because they'd come and they were from down south someplace, and they weren't much for keeping their yard clean and so on. They were good people, but now Mrs. Creighton would go and call on them. It was strange, some of her friendships. And I don't know that in the society of Moscow then, I don't think Mrs. Creighton functioned in that at all. Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Creighton, was he the same way? Did he mix with ordinary people?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Not anymore than just in the store. I think they were very, maybe I shouldn't say, but when Harry was a kid, I know when he worked for Creighton's delivering packages, and he carried their laundry down to their washroom, and that was one of his jobs. Mrs. Creighton was always awfully good to him. He'd go and maybe, she'd always have him come in and she'd have a treat for him, and maybe he'd have his hands in his pockets and she'd tell him, take your hands out of your pockets, you know, and she'd tell him things like that, not to stand with his hands in his pockets. And his hands got rough and she told him how he could avoid those rough hands, was to soap his hands real good, and have someone sprinkle a teaspoon of sugar in them and rub them in it, that'll soften your hands no fooling. And then in a day or two, he'd go back down and get the clean laundry and take it back to Creighton's home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know her?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have an idea of why she mixed the ordinary?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, I never did. It seemed very unusual. Well, she'd stop at our house lots of times. She liked my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why she was more interested in people and people than us?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Probably, I don't know. But I can see her. She was quite big fronted. She wore a beaver coat, a beautiful beaver coat, and she walked quite proud and so on. I can still see her when she'd go by to go up the street to the neighbors.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But as far as mixing in church or social life, I don't believe they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, if you were going to think of some of the leading families in Moscow at the time in social life and church affairs, who would someone be?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, of course, the David family was always prominent. And the HR Smiths and Charlie Shields, he was the hardware man. These people all just happened to be in our church.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And the Watkins, Carol, Riley, brings grandparents.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But, of course, I don't think there were many clubs or anything. There was a club up on the—that was called the Hayu Club, sort of a sewing social club. That was up from near the old David home in that neighborhood. And then in our neighborhood, a woman came in. I don't know how old I was. I was just a little girl. They had a little girl we played together. And she started a neighborhood sewing club. And that was about the extent of it. But you would have maybe two or three couples come in in the evening to your home, and my parents did, and play cards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were their friends? My parents?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes, the people that they would have now. So that they would associate with them socially like that. They were mostly, I think, people that they were quite near in the neighborhood. And I can remember the Nailers used to come and play cards. And, oh, I don't remember. I remember one time my mother and father were having people in to play cards in the evening, and several of my friends. And we always thought when Friday night came, we had to do something, you know, because the next day was Saturday and so on. And we'd go to each other's houses. And I remember that time we'd play games and things and have little refreshments, you know. But at that time, I didn't, I'd invited my friends and probably hadn't consulted my mother. And so the living room was in use, so we had to, I had to have my friends in the kitchen. But we played games in the kitchen. They were all little girls.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But that was one thing. We never had to ask if we were going to a picnic or something. If they were told us to bring so -and -so a cake or sandwich or so on. We could just always say, yes, we can do that. But some of the children had to go home and ask first. And now I thought that was so funny. As I got older, sometimes I made the cakes myself and they weren't too good. But I never had to ask if I could take a cake to the picnic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that, what the difference was? Do you think it was that your parents just wanted your kids to do whatever they wanted to do?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, not do whatever they wanted to do, especially, but they wanted us to feel that we had the privilege of, if we wanted to say we'd take something, that we could take it. That's really what I meant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We didn't have to go home and say, could I take a cake to the picnic Saturday?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: We just knew we could. In that way, they respected your wishes as people. I think so very much. Even though you were still kids. I think so very much.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That sounds like a rather progressive attitude towards kids, too. Well, no. My father was not that way, I was told, with my older brothers and sisters. Even when they were in college and went to parties and dances at the university, they were told to be home at such a time and they had to be home then. They just didn't... But it was different with you. Yes, I knew I wasn't expected, and Morris, too. We knew we weren't expected to stay out too late, but we never had to leave the party before it was over. So I think my father was more tolerant with Morris and I. You know, these parties, you mentioned to Laura the QAE Club, and some of these parties that you went to as a teenager, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the activities that the young people would engage in? Would they be party games?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Party games. Spin the platter and wank them on such games as that. That all the whole group would participate in. Would there be the sorts of things, some of the things that they did in the rural schools, like Happy Is the Miller Boy and... Well, none of those parties. We did that when I was in high school. We had parties. The old building was torn down, and we'd do that in the hall. I know we'd get down in the front hall and Happy Is the Miller Boy. That came back to me when I read Lola's book, because we did that and skipped to Mooloo. But those would be in school, not a party at home. At home, it would be these... Just power games. I don't think we played salad tables and played games like children play. It was always just group.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: They'd put out a whole lot of objects, and you'd go look at them and then write down all you could remember. That sort of thing, too.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Dancing, though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that ever done in the homes?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Of course, too many homes weren't... So our home wasn't a place where we could have danced, only we just didn't dance. We just played games. Until I got in... Well, I can tell you about one experience, dancing. We were having... In our church, there were two classes, the girls passed, the boys passed, and we were all... Wait a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this a story about how you snuck back?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: You told that to Lola. Lola had that. I was thinking I'd go back. I was wondering about that. You snuck back. That was because the Sunday school teacher wouldn't have allowed you to dance. No, he wasn't that way at all. He would have gone along and probably sat and played the piano. But I don't know. We just thought that was fun to do it that way or something. I don't know why we did that. You just didn't want to let him and the other teacher know. Well, he was... That was Dr. Axtell. He was such a good sport. It was a great thing then to...

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: He'd play for us, and this group of girls, teenagers, would stand around the piano and sing all the popular songs, you know. And he just entered right into that.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: He'd sit and play the piano just as long as we wanted to sing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he wouldn't have objected to that?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I don't think he would have. I know he wouldn't have later had his own children because... Well, that makes it seem like you were really playing a prank. Well, I think it wasn't. We thought it was a prank. And I don't know why we thought we had to do it that way. Then you went and got the girl, got the people next door to the chapel. Well, a young woman, Miss Madeline Shields, and she brought her a fiancé. They chaperoned the party. Was this girl Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Day's niece?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That had the party, mm -hmm. And Mrs. Day was not at home at the time, too. But she knew that the party was to be there. Well, that wasn't on the sly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To you kids, was going to the Day's house a big deal? Was it something special?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes. We thought that was something special. Especially if you were asked to eat there. I don't remember. Well, they had more than just a hired girl in the kitchen. But you were served at the dining room table. And most of us weren't used to that, you see. Our meals all served family style. I don't even remember my father ever serving the plates too much. It was just only he used to fix my food when I was little, especially my pancakes. And I didn't want anybody else to fix them with my father because he fixed them. And I just wondered if they'd still taste that good to me. He put a lot of butter and sprinkled sugar in them, poured cream on them. They'd puff up, you know. Oh, I thought that was delicious. That was special for you.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: You know, not long ago, Miss Kirkwood is an artist, you know, and Mr. Westerland. They were out at our place to a party, a talking party. And we got to talking about people painting and so on. And I told them that I just didn't have a bit of that in me. I said, I remember the only picture I painted was a watercolor. And it was a black sailboat on water. And right away, Mr. Westerland said that showed that I was an unhappy child.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, do you like the water?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And I said, no. And that I disliked the water and so on. And since then I thought about that so much, I said that wasn't true. My childhood, he thought that sounded like my father dominated me, that painting. And I said that wasn't true. And since then I thought everybody in the class, the teacher told them to paint that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A black sailboat on water?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I certainly don't see how you can conclude from a painting or something. Well, I don't like water, and I don't enjoy boats too much. But I don't know how he can say that that showed my father. Sounds like armchair psychology. I don't know. And art is psychology anyway. But since then, the first next time I see him, I'm going to bring that to his mind and telling that that was a class, the whole class did the same thing. Maybe it was the teacher who was dominated by his father. That's right. I thought I might come across that painting in all the junk I had, but I didn't. I was in hopes I would so I could show it to Miss Kirkwood and Mr. Westerland. You know, talking about the people who were well to do in Moscow, I'm wondering about the extent, if any, of social distance that other people felt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For instance, did your family feel a social distance between you and the shield, say, and the children?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no. There was no... I don't think that was true. And in those days, they did have a historical club. I spoke up. His three daughters were all quite socially involved in things. And he was gruff and crude and spoke, but they were about the best dress girls in Moscow. And he was the same kind of a father about wanting his family. And I know Marjorie, it was a little groupos, and we got out on honor. If you made certain grades, you didn't have to take the final examinations. There was a system in the Moscow School, and we called it getting out on honor. And he always gave her five dollars when she got out on honor. And so we'd all go and buy food, stuff we like, bought from the store, you know. Dill pickles like they had in the store, not like we had at home. And ice cream, and we'd buy such stuff and always had it at their house in the kitchen. But she always got five dollars, and the rest of us did that. When she got her five dollars, you would all go out and spend the money together. That's very nice of her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Zumhoff?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yeah. Colorful fellow. And then I had another friend, Nappa Naylor, and we had it right where our house is on 3rd Street. There was a little corner grocery. A widow started it, and she did sewing in her store. And maybe I'd have a nickel to spend, but Nappa always had a dime. And so we could buy more penny candy, but she shared it right along with me, because she had a dime and I only had a nickel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that even if the kids moved in the same circles, that the parents would move in somewhat different circles, depending on their wealth?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, I don't think it depended on the wealth or anything, but people did things more as neighborhoods. I think the social evolved around the neighborhood, their social life. And as far as now, the Shields probably had as much money as I'm sure as anyone. And there were the two boys in the family that were in my group. But Mrs. Shields is a very, I don't think she ever entered into any social life. She was just a quiet type of woman, a little woman that stayed over at her house.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, I don't think the money had anything, the amount of wealth or whatever you want to call it, the family had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did determine then social problems?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I just don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would make a family?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: A family or an individual somebody that was... I really don't know, because I can think of some who didn't have very much, who were just... I just don't know what the... I don't think there was... The model of what the family had, had anything to do with what group circle they mixed in. I don't believe it did. It sounds like the church was a really very sensual thing. It was a very social, much social thing. And of course, when my brothers and sisters, my older brothers and sisters were young people, the church was quite the center. They had an organization, Christian Endeavor, and that was much of the social life for the young people who belonged to the Presbyterian Church. It was less so by the time that you left Illinois. That's right. Except for these Sunday school parties we threw on our own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had the kind of parties that you had this QAE club and that sort of thing, was that sort of taking the place of the church?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Probably. Because I know when I came along, I didn't want any of that Christian Endeavor stuff and so on. They tried to keep it going in our church, but it just died out. That didn't appeal to me. I don't know. Maybe because we devolved our own social life this other way. You mentioned the missionary society to Laura and that they combined, which was a good move.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But was the missionary society separate in what it was trying to do? Was it trying to raise money for the foreign missions?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, especially. So the people would be informed. They sent money to the church board to go for foreign missions and home missions, they called it and so on. But there weren't too many women. Many of the women in the church liked the Ladies Aid Society because even though they always had a religious type of service and everything, it was more social, you see. And they did the things like the ice cream socials and things, raised money to get some new dishes and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the missionary society do?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: The women studied the different missions that we helped support, but Mrs. Garmley thought that was all wrong.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it wasn't as much fun for the women?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, the one who were in the missionary society, they were more serious about what they were doing. It just didn't involve so many women.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: So she was pretty smart too. Because that way they put some of the missionary work into the Ladies Aid Society.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be true to say that the women were the people who were primarily involved in the church activities and that the men were much less so?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I mean now outside of just the Sunday service. You know what I'm wondering is whether the women were really the people that kept the church going. Oh yes, yes, absolutely. Then later, I think, well, probably ministers came who tried to have men's groups, but they never got across. In some way, you know, they'd die out. They'd go good for a while. But then when they started having a midweek, like with the potluck supper and men and women both, but then men would come to that, you see. Now they're trying to start, and Dean Krause, they had one this morning, but they put it so early because if the professors come, they have to be free to get to school. A 645 breakfast. Harry went the other one they had, but he didn't even read it on the Sunday bulletin. I think they were going to have that. He said he wouldn't be going. He didn't think after the last one because I think they dismissed it. It's just a continental breakfast, and that wouldn't bother him. In fact, I think it's just kind of a bull session or something of that sort of thing. I don't think they've been trying to get that started in our church of the mania. It would be fair to say that the church in the early days, the church you belonged to really determined a large extent of who you would spend your social life with. Maybe that and it combined with the neighborhood that you lived in. Of course, as I remember, our neighborhood, we lived in what they called Sweet Town. They were all Lutherans and many Methodists. They didn't involve what your religion was in your neighborhood group. Of course, now in those days, men went to lodges. They did.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: My father was an odd fellow and he was a woodman of the world. I think he felt he was a member that he should go to those things. Most of them had ladies' auxiliaries, and then they'd get together for suppers and parties on special occasions. I think lodges played a big part in the men's social life. I'm sure they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother in the ladies' auxiliaries?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the groups that he was in?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Al McKeeling's parents, I know, were woodmen of the world. Let me see, the women of the woodcraft were the women. I was taken as a child with my parents to parties at Al McKeeling's house because they had one of the big homes. Now, as far as Alma ever entering into any of this, I never remember her at all. Of course, Alma's, I shouldn't say this, we've always been kind of a loner. When we were in high school then, her brother was in my class, Ralph, and Ms. Lauder used to have parties for us quite often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Connected with the lodge or just the school?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no, just our Ralph's class, school parties. In fact, I remember after we'd been out of high school a year, I was in school at Lewiston, and Ralph had a party when we came home. She'd just gone to so much trouble. I remember one thing, it was supper, I think she had us too. She made mashed sweet potatoes and made little, she was quite artistic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you ever in Alma's home?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Never in something. Well, Mrs. Lauder and, oh, she'd make, I can remember one thing they had was a section of a log with a bark on it. She'd varnished it and had a painting in it, you know, that sort of thing. She was great on making something like that. But for this party, she'd molded these little animals some way, of this mashed sweet potato. I don't even remember what we had for the rest of the supper, but I just remember those little things she had formed and put in the oven, you know, and heated up the butter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that party, would you have played games or would you have danced?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, we didn't dance because there were so many more girls than there were boys. But of course, at the school parties, part of the girls would have to be the partner, you see, because in my class there were six boys and fourteen girls in high school. I don't know why the proportion was like that. So when we'd march around with the Miller boy and so on, part of the girls would have to take the gents part. Ladies step forward and the gents fall back. So like at the Keeling's house, it would have been, or a water house, it would have been talking to me. Yeah, singing. We used to like to sing so much for Miller kids. There was a lot of that. And I can remember with my older brother and my sisters, that was a lot of when the young people would get together. Somebody would play and everybody would sing. The Rampsdee family, they were in our neighborhood. They were all good singers. And Mrs. Oberg, Allen Rampsdee, used to play the piano for singing. That was quite the thing, to sing around the piano.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know whether your father felt close to his Lodge brothers and that he would help them if they needed help?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh yes, I think there was that feeling. It would be a special feeling that you wouldn't just have for any of them. I think so. I think so. I think maybe the Odd Fellows and some of those things are like that today. Now I think a bigger Lodge like the Alps and the Moose and so on, I don't know what they're like. But I think the Odd Fellows is still that kind of a group in Moscow. It's smaller and they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Sweet Town have a character of its own at that time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: They call it Sweet Town and I wonder if it was it really of its own character.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a community then?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, it wasn't. There were a lot of Scandinavian people that lived there but there were a lot of others too. It's just happened that the majority who had homes in that part of the town were Scandinavian. But the characters, well, of course, farther down our street, Lynn Avenue, Wild Avie was down there toward the bridge. He was the character. Then we had a Negro family who lived down that way, the McCrae's. And of course, I think Jimmy McCrae was in my room for a while at school. And my brother would always tease me, my oldest brother, about Jimmy McCrae, you know. Of course, I'd get very upset when he'd do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He'd tease you how?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That you were a friend of your... Jimmy's girlfriend or something. I don't ever remember even walking along with Jimmy going to school or anything. That was just a way to kid me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you kids were in the same class, do you think that the kids realized that he was a different color from the others?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes, but he wasn't, as I remember, Jimmy wasn't in school very long. But as I remember, they were never pushed aside or anything. Well, when I was thinking of character, I was really thinking of whether there was a different feeling about that area than saying other parts of town. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it any better or worse off of financial in other parts of town?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Many of those Scandinavian men weren't professional men or anything, but their children went to college and everything. I don't know. No. Allen Ramsted's grandfather, they had a big place. I know he used to sell cherries, and he'd lay carpets, and whether he made all of his living that way or not, I don't know.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But his sons all went on to do well, and they all had... I don't know whether they were all college graduates or not, but they had the opportunity to go to school.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Of course, the Naylor family that I speak of, they had big farms out here throughout the town, and they still owned them.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But they didn't go on after high school to college. They just started farming the boys. And they had money, too. He was one of the wealthiest men in the county, Mr. Naylor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that a lot of people just didn't value going to colleges at that time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I don't know how it was. I know my father wanted all his children to go to college, but evidently the Naylor's didn't feel that... Well, Chris farming traditionally didn't require that. No. And they had begun to acquire quite a lot of land. And Mr. Naylor was the sole insurance, and he was a great one to sit in the office and put his feet up and talk to people, you know. In the early days, he was sheriff, and they were prominent in the county, and they were prominent in the Rebekah's and Oddfellows. In those days, they had the GAR, Grand Army, they were prominent in that. They were early -day settlers here. But they didn't move what you'd call in the better circles, although they were always accepted and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would the better circle or the best circles be those which would have the days in the McConnells and those people in them?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, you said Shields didn't. Mrs. Shields didn't, did her. I'm thinking of who the better circle would be. Well, that's kind of hard for me to say because...

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I don't know. They kind of thought of up around the day home on farther east, the David home around the Catholic Church. Whether that was kind of nobbiel or not. I don't know whether they thought it that way.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who lived there besides the Davids in the days right now?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And Old Smith and the Carruthers, D .U .S. the doctor I spoke of. His daughter was one of my best friends. But still, right in that same block lived the harness maker, Weber. See, there was no distinction of...

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And I think as far as groups were concerned, it was just more within the neighborhood of a group of congenial people who would get together.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And then the Lodges and I think many of those people didn't enter into that Lodge business. And then the Church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father identify much with Moscow businessmen in general?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No. No, he didn't do that. He didn't belong to the Chamber of Commerce when they started in Moscow. He just didn't go for that sort of thing.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: He just...he...he just didn't because I...and Rotary and that sort of thing. He just didn't go for that.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And when I was a kid, I used to think, well, Dad ought to enter into those things, you know, more. But he...I don't know whether it was pressure of time or what, that he just didn't enter into those things. Or I think, too, maybe he felt that they kind of set a standard that...and he wasn't going to conform to a standard of... He had his business. He...I really don't know his feeling, but I think he felt he had to kind of conform to something that he didn't want to conform to. A way of behaving or... Well, no. ...or a certain way of dealing with customers and that kind of thing. Entering into the Main Street sort of affair and all that kind of... Well, maybe, too, he had...if he had his life pretty filled up with his other concerns... Well, he did. And his business, he put in long, long hours at his shop and office and... He worked more than eight hours a day at his shop. Oh, yes.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: During...before...of course, when his business got big and he'd employ many men. But I can remember the month of May was a very trying month in our home as far as... We all hated to have Dad work such long as he'd even work Sundays because he never wanted to disappoint anyone on having their monument for Memorial Day. And I can remember way back when things like strawberries and things first came on the market, his appetite wouldn't be very good and my mother would buy strawberries. And none of us expected to eat them. They were for Dad. We wanted him to have them. For May. Uh -huh. For a month of May. We were down until after Memorial Day because the house would be upset. We all tried to shield him because he worked so very hard and... Spring house cleaning. That's interesting. So the house was really done to suit your father's needs to get up to his... time because if there was any painting or anything to be done, it was never even brought up until after Memorial Day because...

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: The house just wasn't...the house wasn't to be...

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: in a mess or anything because that would bother Dad. Everyone in the family wanted to make it easy for him because he was working so hard. You described the stone to Laura, but the one point that I wanted to be clear about was that he would get the shape, the monument sent in, in the shape that it would be. But he would do the carving and the lettering on the monument. And sometimes put the little rose bud there. But I think by the time he established his business in Moscow, most of the design was on like the roses or whatever, the lilies or class pans or so on. And he just did the engraving.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there anyone else in town that was doing it?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh no. Only one around this territory. There was one in Colfax. But my dad and one in Lewiston. But my dad had a terribly big territory all over the prairie and as far as Yakima, I guess. He'd send out agents, he called them, with their design kits, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about delivering of the monuments? Would people have to come and pick up the mop or would he deliver them?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh no, no, no. He sent them. He sent them right there in the cemetery. And the stones that are all standing up straight out there in the cemetery my father put there because he put a big foundation under the granite box. But of course they don't even use those granite blocks anymore. But they'd be a big cement foundation. He had a man to do that, but he was right there when that stone was set. So he and another man would go out together. So he would travel around a great deal. Well yes, in the spring of the year there was quite a bit of going. And let me see, even in a gray, you know, horse hold, of course I think like stones they shipped out, they would be shipped out and he'd go and, or else the agents usually could set stones too in these houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After this early, really hard times, did he find that it was a steadily good business for him?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh yes, until at my dad's, after my dad's death is when they started with this marker, business ground markers, you know. And he, he wouldn't have liked that. He liked monuments. And he liked white marble.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: He sold lots of granite and various kinds, but he just loved white marble. And it's very poor for outdoor. Our monuments on our lot off the cemetery, white marble, they're so stained now, you know, and you can't scrub the lettering out anymore. It's, they're rough and because of weather, they wouldn't weather. What was it about white marble, do you think, that really appealed to the cleanness of the... a heavier looking material and of course, I think my father really had a very refined taste. Well, I think that linen tablecloth maybe, and his clothes, how well he kept them and brushed them and so on. He wouldn't have anymore started off to work in the morning without polished shoes and he would have flown. And they'd just get dusty down there in the days when he did the carving himself, you know, because he'd blow off the marble dust, you know.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That must mean that a lot of the stones, the older stones that I've seen in the cemeteries on the ridges, were his. Sometimes down on the little corner some places would be G .H .M. Often people ask him to put his initials down. I know that quite a few out in the cemetery out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that when you were growing up that your family ever wanted for anything that they, that it was needed?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, no I don't. I have, I know it was tough going lots of times. Of course I was the youngest one and I didn't realize maybe how tough it was going. But because things after my birth and so on started on the up for him more. But I have an account book and I think I told Laura about him sitting down every night and putting the day's expenditures. And like in that book it's very interesting for him to me to read them. And I haven't decided what I'm going to do with that book yet either. But wife, a dollar and a half for goods. Now maybe she went down and bought calico for dresses or something, I don't know. But that was the way it was. Mabel's shoes, 25 cents. She probably dripped her shoes you know. She was the one in the family who was always hired on her shoes. But every night that was all as a separate item. It wasn't told up for the day's expenditures.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he keep track too exactly?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: He must have kept track of what he spent on the marble. Well that was all done at the shop you see. That was separate.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: You know when you talk about people that had real wealth in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were any of them ostentatious about it? Were there any people that liked to show that they had money?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well probably Mrs. Creighton was as ostentatious as anyone with her dress and being aloof. But still she'd go see these people you see.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: What I was wondering was if that was frowned on to try to be showing during that time. I had the idea that it's become a lot more acceptable perhaps in the recent years. I don't think so. I don't think so.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I don't. I can't think of. Of course I refer more to the church than anything but there were people now I can think of one family who had quite a bit and they were very simple about their dress. And didn't enter into well I don't think we had a high society in Moscow at that when I was a child.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the people were when you were a child very serious about their religion at that time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That it had a really it played the religious part of it itself now apart from the social part. Oh yes I think so. My mother was very religious and Mrs. F .A. David and Mrs. H .R. Smith. They were all very religious and they went to the mid weekly prayer meeting and their husbands never went but they did. But I think it played a big part in the religious life and the men supported the church financially and would go to church but the women really I think made it because I know I remember I was just a little girl and my mother had this terrible surgery. That was very severe and probably for that time quite a hard thing for the doctors to do. It was prayer meeting night and they turned the prayer meeting into a prayer service for my mother. Those friends the Davids and Mrs. Smith and McConnells and so on. Now you see we were talking about McConnells. Now Mrs. the Governor's wife was a very quiet little woman. She never entered into any social life at all. She was like Mrs. Shields. I think her family came first.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I don't remember her too. I remember her because she used to come to church but of course the Governor was the pompous one in that family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the women believe in bringing gifts when they would come visiting?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Was that a part of the... No I don't think so. You mean like flowers and candy or... Yes or maybe in time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about in time of trouble?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well now there's more. I think people help more now today because I think I don't suppose there's a group in Moscow that if something happens to someone in that group or even in the family or something that they don't send in a meal. I think there's a great deal more of that. Then there used to be. Of course Martin, Mickey always tells about the time they came to Moscow and after they got here was Thanksgiving. They lived across the street from us on the end of the house. They had nothing but packing boxes and so on sitting around. Martin always tells me yet about me bringing my mother sending over a Thanksgiving dinner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wondered, I said to Martin a long ago, why do you suppose Mama just didn't have you all come over to our house and leave?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well he said probably my mother wouldn't have gone because they weren't unpacked and she probably wouldn't have gone. Not because of not knowing us but because she felt they weren't... Weren't ready to go anywhere. Probably didn't have clean clothes. So I don't know whether that was why I don't even know whether my mother consulted them or I think she said over words she was sending over the dinner. But of course Martin thinks that was the most wonderful thing that ever happened to them because... It was quite a warm welcome that's for sure. Must have given a very good feeling to Moscow. Well I think it did, probably.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: You know there's one thing from your childhood that you had mentioned the lore of it but we never did put down at all and that was about some of the ways that you kids had a good time in grade school. Oh yes we had such fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you have fun there?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well this same group of little girls just like a bop up from the old high school we went to the fifth grade I think it was in the old high school. They had a first bell of 830 and then a last bell at five and so nine and the tardy bell at nine you see just a few taps for the tardy bell. And we'd go up to this every noon we congregate up to these steps of two blocks away above the McConnell mansion there. And then when the last bell ring would ring we'd just tear down the hill to school to get in before the tardy bell rang. See just kind of a I don't know what you'd call it. But we just love to do things like that. And we'd not only do it once we'd do it time after time to get me in our seats before the tardy bell rang. But not a second sooner than you had to be. No no. And then in the early grade, it's third grade, I went to the old Russell School which was where on the same grounds was the Russell School and that was a wooden building. And the walk in front of the mansion there, Governor McConnell's place was a high sidewalk because it was the street was down in and so on. And we'd play little girls play house there under the sidewalk. Every noon we took our lunch to school and we'd hurry over to play house. Leaf dishes and all pretend stuff you know rose hips and underneath the side underneath the sidewalk. We were quite secluded down there and we thought but we did that all the time when the weather was good.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: And my one friend I know in the fall of the year, we liked to play like that, pretend, all pretend when we were alone you know. We didn't need anything but leaves and this and that and the other to pretend we were either running a bake shop or something you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In school, could you play to fool around or was it was it very a rather strict situation?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh no, I think we were all pretty good in school. We didn't come. And I think, I don't remember how that year we played so much under the sidewalk. I don't remember that the teacher was especially cross but everybody behaved. And I think the worst thing that could happen to you would be sent the cloakroom you know, be banished from the room for a while. No hitting of the kids. I don't remember of a teacher ever hitting. I don't remember that I ever knew of a teacher giving us banking. They had a separate class for each grade at that time when we went to school. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that you learned quite a bit in school?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think our education was splendid. We learned to be good readers. We could spell and we could go up the blackboard and do problems. We knew our time stables. I think we had a splendid education, a three hours education and I think it was splendid. Some children didn't do so well because they probably were children who needed a little extra help and they didn't get it because the teachers didn't have time to be individual. No, they didn't think about this. Some children's ability not like they do now to need help like in reading some special help or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think they taught to the average?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think so. And of course there were some who were always superior.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I used to love to be asked, Mom would come and visit every whole few months and I was very proud when she'd come. I was always glad if we had reading and the teacher'd ask me to go up in front and stand and read. I just thought that was wonderful if I could stand up and read with a paragraph before the class.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she visiting just to see how the school was being taught?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I think so. She was interested in how we were. And then I think partly because I just thought it was awful nice for my mother to come to school. You mentioned that people visited by bringing their calling cards the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It made me wonder, were these visits rather formal visits?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, yes, I think probably they were calls, they called them. And they did say to all night, it wasn't come spend the afternoon or anything like that. You'd probably make several calls in the afternoon. Or a newcomer, you always called and left your card. So, new people in town. I wonder, so these would be visits usually between people that didn't know each other too well. Yes, newcomers coming to town. And I think there was a feeling that they wanted to make them feel welcome, that they belonged and that they knew somebody and help them get acquainted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people still come and call on your, say on your mother?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh yes, yes, the people would come and call. They used to say that they had the women who lived over on the University of Aldrich's and Little's and so on. That they had the day that they were at home so that a woman coming from the town side would find them at home. They did go to call and vice versa. But I don't think my mother ever called days like that. Of course we lived out farther and probably the people who lived in a group on the hill there did that more. A certain day of the week would be at home. This would be a call, just one person would be there. Yeah, put on your gloves and so on. I really wonder what that purpose that really served besides for the new people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a way just for people to get out?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, no, I think they felt they had a duty to help these people get acquainted. And you kind of sized them up at the first call. Decided if you wanted to see them again. I think that was what you do now too. Yes, indeed. But then it was rather, it sounds rather formal. Well, I think you could say they were formal calls. I was thinking about that when I read what you told Laura. I'm wondering what it is that people would talk about in a formal situation. Well, probably just small talk.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Of course, I can remember after I was married, women whom I knew had known came to call. And then sort of all the social life, they'd start inviting you to their luncheons and things, you see. Because you were grown up married, so to speak, ready to go to parties and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would these be your contemporaries or older?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, older women would do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they already know you pretty well?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes, but they could, for you were married. Of course, I think probably I didn't pay so much attention to them when I was growing up because I was still having my good time and so on. But you did appreciate after you were married and they came to call, you felt you were part of the society of the town and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there calls formal too?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, I can remember so well. When we were first married, there were no apartments. The only house that was available, my father didn't want me to live in it. He had a long stairway in the back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long stairway?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Long stairway straight down to the backyard. He thought I might have fallen out. My dad was like that. So we took these rooms, the only place we could find to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was Carol O 'Riari's grandmother as what kids?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: So she let us have the use of the Paula, which she always called a Paula. There were two bedrooms back of it. After her family were away from home, she kept rumors. But they were vacant at that time, so we took both bedrooms. One we used as a bedroom, and the other we made into a kitchen. There was no water in it. There was a faucet outside. Chris Carroll wasn't a home man. I guess she wasn't married yet either, but she was away at school. But we took, I can remember, a table from our house, and we got our electric stove hadn't come. So Washington Water Power loaned us one. It was just a makeshift sort of lighthouse keeping proposition. I remember one day after we were in the summer, we had both Harry's family and my father and my sister to dinner. I can remember so well Mrs. Whittier, the banker's wife, said, as we were going out of church or something, I said I had to hurry home, I was going to have company. She said, I didn't suppose you even knew how to boil water. But I remember she and Mrs. Simpson, who lived up right across from the old Butterfield house up there, Ms. Whittier did, and Mrs. Simpson lived all around the corner. They had come to call on me after Harry and I had, or Mary, moved in there. I don't think I was very dignified about their call. I curled up in the big chair that was there, and they made a brief call that they didn't seem to hold it against me. In other words, you wouldn't take it very seriously. No, I didn't take it very seriously, but they left their cards. They were still observing, I trust them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the saying that she didn't imagine you even knew how to boil water?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That sounds like a bit of an insult to me. No, it wasn't because she knew the way that I'd never done that sort of thing in our home. Because after my sisters were gone, we kept help. As I say, I mowed the lawn, and well, I'd been away to school several years. I don't know how she knew, but she actually, it was the truth. I didn't really know how to cook because I'd never been taught. I never died to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it for you to learn? Did you learn just after you married?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yeah, and I can remember we didn't have any facilities to keep anything. So every morning I'd dress up and go down to the butcher shop and buy some kind of meat. I thought I was being economical by just buying a couple of chops or something, you see. I didn't realize that that was the most expensive way for us to live. But I also learned, well, we weren't there too long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have any fridge, any fridge in the house?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No. So it was just day -to -day living practically. It's not so different from the way lots of kids start out. The way we have to do that. Yes, it was, just from out like Gibson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't stay there?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: No, we weren't there very long, just a few months through the summer. And then we expected to get in this man was building a house to get in it, and we were to have it. But we didn't get in until about Christmas time. And that's the first time we used our furniture that we bought when we were married. We'd been storing the store all that time, David's store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first meet Harry?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, we were kids. I was in the eighth grade, and he was in high school. That's the first time, well, I remember him before that, because he played a guitar, and they had a mandolin club here, and his sister played the mandolin and several other young people, kids. And they'd be asked to go around and entertain. I used to go with this Naylor family to lodge things, and these kids would come. So I knew Harry from that. That's the first time ever. But anyway, I didn't, he was so bashful. And I was in eighth grade, and Mae Costello, who I knew real well, was in Harry's class. And she brought this note to me, and they were having a skating, roller skating party. And Harry asked me to go with him. And it was a group of boys who weren't very big. They called themselves the Runt Club. They had a little club, and so this was their party. I don't know how he happened to ask me, but Mae brought the note asking me to go at the party with Harry. These were boys that were not big. They were small for their age and inside. The Runt Club. There were about a dozen of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you went?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh yes. Couldn't skate at all. Of course, Harry had a terrible time. I'd hardly skate, and I fell down. But he wanted to appear quite grown up, but it was in the winter. And when he took me home, I remember so well, he slept at our gate. We still had to—no, he didn't have a fence then. It had been taken down. And he'd taken his father's watch to appear quite grown up, you know, in his pocket, big watch. And it fell out in the snow when he slept, flew out of his pocket. And my dad found it the next morning in the snow. I can't remember about returning it. Well, I returned it to him, but I don't think his mother ever knew that he'd taken his father's watch. He'd grown up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like him right off the bat, or was it later?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, no. He was so bashful, I wasn't too impressed. And he never had been in this group that I'd been with all through junior high, upper grades. When we had our QA, he had never been in it. I never even—we just hadn't been thrown together at all. And then I just don't remember much of him after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this QA club, was this also mostly girls?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, no, no, no. There was many boys in that. Was that a neighborhood club, or was it— No, because I lived way over here, and the girl who started it, her mother was the one who started the sewing club in our neighborhood. They were kind of organization -minded, I guess. No, they were just a bunch of kids that were friendly, kind of. And maybe a little cliquey, too. I think so. From school mostly, or just some certain kids that knew other kids that— No, from school, and we weren't all even in the same grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were all your close friends in the club at the time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes. It was cliquey. I'm sure it was a cliquey group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were invited to belong?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Wasn't this sort of thing, where anybody that showed was— Oh, no. No, you—I think the group was formed, and we just stuck to our group.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But I think we were cliquey, which was too bad. But of course they didn't have a youth center or anything in those days. I don't know whether kids have—I know they have their special friends, because our grandson does in jealousy. But I don't know whether they click up like that now or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how old were you when you started, what grade?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, I think we were about in the sixth grade. We were pretty young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did it continue for very long?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, several years. We didn't have it after we were in high school.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: But we didn't. We always went as a crowd. There were only just one or two kids that would pair off as boy and girl. We just went as a bunch.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: I was thinking about when you did go to college. You said, Laura, you didn't really like Home Ec. You told her about that jar that you broke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just that you hadn't—it just wasn't suited to your temperament?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: That's right. That wasn't my temperament. But my father thought girls could be teachers, and he thought Home Ec was a good thing for me to take. So I registered in Home Ec. But I sure couldn't stick it out. That just wasn't my field.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went to Kendrick and taught, what did you teach there?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, that was funny. I finished normal in Lewiston, but I took physical education. My brother -in -law was on the school board, and Kendrick and the teacher had the teacher in the eighth grade, I think. I had to have a pandectomy. This was in the Thanksgiving vacation. So Frank taught me in the teaching school for the rest of the semester. They were just starting a Home Ec department in the Kendrick school. I taught some classes in English and I don't know what else. I can't remember. Grammar, of course, they had separate from reading and so on. I don't know what I taught. I can't remember what I taught. And the Home Ec, well, their equipment hadn't come. And if it hadn't been there, I'd have been sunk. But as it was, I'd had a year of Home Ec in high school. In fact, when I was in eighth grade, they started some Home Ec here in Moscow, and I was in that. And then when I was a senior in high school, I decided, I took it just for kind of... High school. Like, I gave the class on what they, the way they taught foods in those days, you know, the food you should eat and what foods contain, so on and so on. So, I mean, if you'd had the equipment, you had to do canning and that sort of thing, you wouldn't have trouble. Oh, I'd have been sunk, yes. Or even had to establish this kitchen. It was just a room with tables in it as it was, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, you stayed there just for the semester?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Uh -huh. But I did do, I taught the girls basketball on the side after school, because that was my field. And they didn't have anything like that, so I took that on and got the girls interested in doing things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you enjoy that experience? Did you like it?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Oh, yes. Well, my sister lived there, of course. And yes, I enjoyed it. There was no social life at all, but I came home usually on the weekends. Of course, I was going to the area in those days, and I came home and we did things. But I remember there was another young man that he wasn't teaching. I don't know what he did. Green, his name was. And he'd come and see me in the evenings, or somebody would invite us to their house or something. But that was all social life there was during the week. There wasn't anything. Of course, I was young. I got kind of fed up with just that. And my sister's children were low, and it wasn't very exciting. Well, you know, when you said that you would have been interested maybe in being a doctor, that was really beyond the pale for me. Yeah, or my father was concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it sounds like then the opportunities for a woman to go ahead and do what you wanted to do were kind of limited at that time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, it was opening up for women to do other things, but my father just didn't think that some things were fitting proper for young women to do. Being a teacher was fine. And he was coming to a place then that if I'd studied, gone to a business college or something, that would have been all right with him. But he didn't think some of these things were. That was nothing for a young woman to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a real desire of yours at the time?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes, well, I tell you. No, I really wanted to. But the woman who was the head of our department there at the Normal had broken into her medical school education to earn money. And she was one of the first women doctors in New York City, because I saw an article just a year or two ago in some magazine, and she'd retired. I wish I had tried to look her up when our son was in medical school back there, because I think she was still a practicing doctor then in New York City in the fifties. But this article I read in some magazine was Miss Dorothy Baucher, was one of the first doctors in New York. You told Laura about her, how she taught you, but it sounds like you had a great deal of admiration for her. Oh, yes, I did. And that may have influenced me too. We all just adored her. But she was down to business as far as our teaching was concerned. And she taught the subjects in physical education of we had anatomy and physiology and kinesiology and of course in bacteriology. Pretty technical. Well, you see, she was in medical school. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, it was enjoyable to you to live in Lewiston too, to be in there and go on the way to school. There were very few boys in the normal school, and there were a few boys around Lewiston that always were around to take you someplace. But the dormitory was really like the legislature said, it was just sort of a finishing school for girls because we had wonderful social life in the dormitory. And the food was just these big Saturday night dinners and so on, you know, formal dinners. And I don't remember who was governor, but he thought the legislature ought to cut back the funds from that girl's finishing school in Lewiston. Well, this was not a sorority, but the dormitory forever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you been interested in a sorority up in Moscow here?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Well, no. No, I hadn't. And my sisters were sorority girls, but I didn't. But down there in Lewiston, I remember they had attempted some of the girls who lived in the dorm and some of the Lewiston girls took former sorority. And they wanted to get a house and live in a house. And they asked me, and I liked it too well in the dorm. I couldn't see, and so I didn't accept the invitation. But they never did get a group together to have a house and live in a... So the dormitory really was a nice situation for the girls then. And we had a little living room. They were sweets, two girls, with a fireplace. And we'd go out and we could get some wood from the furnace room. We'd get it from the man. And otherwise we'd try to find the stickers too long for somebody's fans someplace. So we could have a fire. And we had our sugar and chocolate, but we'd steal butter to put in our fudge, take it up in our napkin ring and so on. And we'd have slumber parties. Yes!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So slumber parties too while you were in college?

speaker: Clarice Moody Sampson
words: Yes. That was quite the thing, our slumber parties down there. But sometimes the dean of women who lived in the hall would come and shush us up.
